title: IP Fragmentation
description: Splitting IP packets that exceed the MTU of a network link into smaller fragments for transmission.
fullExplanation: Every network link has an MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit) — the largest packet it can carry. Ethernet MTU is 1500 bytes. If an IP packet is larger than the MTU, IPv4 routers fragment it into smaller pieces. Each fragment has the same IP ID, a fragment offset, and the MF (More Fragments) flag set (except the last fragment). The destination host reassembles fragments. IPv6 does NOT allow router fragmentation — only the source host can fragment, and Path MTU Discovery (PMTUD) is used to find the minimum MTU along the path.

fragmentationFields.description: 16-bit ID shared by all fragments of the same original packet
Don't Fragment — if set, router drops packet and sends ICMP 'Fragmentation Needed'
More Fragments — set on all fragments except the last
Position of this fragment in the original packet (in 8-byte units)

pmtud.description: Path MTU Discovery — finds minimum MTU along path to avoid fragmentation
steps: Source sends packet with DF (Don't Fragment) bit set
If router can't forward without fragmenting, it drops packet and sends ICMP Type 3 Code 4 (Fragmentation Needed) with next-hop MTU
Source reduces packet size and retries
Process repeats until packet reaches destination

keyFacts: Ethernet MTU: 1500 bytes. Jumbo frames: 9000 bytes (data centers).
IPv4 fragmentation done by routers. IPv6 fragmentation only by source host.
Reassembly only at destination — intermediate routers don't reassemble
Fragment timeout: if all fragments don't arrive within 60s, packet discarded
PMTUD Black Hole: firewalls blocking ICMP break PMTUD — TCP connections hang
TCP MSS (Maximum Segment Size): negotiated in SYN to avoid IP fragmentation
IP fragmentation attacks: overlapping fragments can confuse IDS/firewalls
